title: A spectral model of power-law decay in natural and engineered systems
authors: Balázs Sándor, Márk Honti, Henrique Santos Lima
abstract: We present a first-principles spectral mechanism for the emergence of nonextensive $q$-exponential dilution and power-law relaxation in non-ideal transport systems. By modeling an incompletely mixed reactor as a layered diffusion matrix with an absorbing boundary, we demonstrate that macroscopic power-law tails depend on the geometric interaction between the initial tracer placement and the domain's boundary configuration. For a one-dimensional system, an asymmetric, volumetrically distributed initial concentration profile projects onto the low-wavenumber eigenmodes, generating an emergent Gamma distribution of relaxation rates; at an infinitesimal boundary layer thickness ($Δz \to 0$), this profile yields the nonextensive $q$-exponential decay function exactly across the entire temporal domain with $q = 5/3$. Extended to $d$ dimensions under a highly localized, boundary-adjacent singular initial condition, the resulting scaling exponents and corresponding $q$ values depend explicitly on the spatial configuration of the absorbing boundaries. However, in the one-dimensional limit ($d=1$), these distinct initial states and boundary formulations intersect, rendering the $q=5/3$ exponent geometrically invariant. Our approach establishes a clear connection between linear diffusion transport and nonextensive statistical mechanics, showing how heavy-tailed transport can be derived from boundary geometry and spectral dimensionality.
summary: This work develops a theoretical framework showing that power-law decay in complex transport processes can be derived from fundamental principles of linear diffusion and spectral analysis. By analyzing the low-wavenumber behavior of the system's eigenmodes, the authors connect classical diffusion directly to nonextensive statistical mechanics, yielding a universal entropic index $q=5/3$ in one dimension.
main_problem: To derive a first-principles spectral mechanism explaining how nonextensive q-exponential dilution and power-law relaxation emerge in linear, non-ideal transport systems.
main_result: The analysis establishes that the asymptotic decay rate is governed by the system's boundary geometry and spectral dimensionality, leading to an emergent entropic index $q=5/3$ in the 1D limit.
method: Spectral analysis of a layered diffusion matrix, involving taking the continuum limit (sum $	o$ integral) over low-wavenumber eigenmodes.
model_or_system: The model uses a layered diffusion system with an absorbing boundary, mathematically analogous to an incompletely mixed reactor or transport process on a semi-infinite domain. The dynamics are governed by a linear master equation ($ rac{dC}{dt} = AC$).
key_observables: Macroscopic relaxation decay $C(t) \propto t^{- eta}$ and the generalized q-exponential function; boundary flux/concentration $C_1(t)$.
important_parameters: The entropic index $q$, spatial dimension $d$, and the low-wavenumber scaling of modal weights ($w(k) \propto k^2$).
main_assumptions: The system is modeled by a linear transport operator, and the analysis relies on taking the continuum limit ($n 	o \infty$) in the spectral decomposition.
figures_summary: Figures compare numerical simulations of boundary concentration decay $C_1(t)$ using three methods (q-exponential fit, continuous analytical solution, discrete sum), showing convergence to the predicted power-law scaling.
paper_structure: The paper develops from defining the spectral mechanism for relaxation in layered systems, deriving asymptotic laws based on dimensional analysis ($d$), and finally demonstrating the invariance of the key exponent ($q=5/3$) in the 1D limit across different initial conditions.
why_interesting: While focused on classical diffusion, the rigorous connection between linear spectral theory (eigenmodes) and nonextensive statistical mechanics provides a powerful mathematical framework applicable to open quantum systems where dissipation or boundary effects are crucial.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
